commentary: Five activities this week against last week's twelve is a drop so steep it deserves a raised eyebrow, but your body clearly needed the exhale after that absolutely unhinged run-swim-lift-swim-lift-swim marathon you pulled off the week before. The Saturday run held its distance at 13.3 kilometres but your heart rate climbed to 155 against last week's 149, which means you were working harder for a similar effort — classic sign of a body still paying its debts. Prince Alfred showed up twice in the pool this week, both sessions tidy at 1500 metres and a calm 130 bpm, though neither touched the 2000-metre sets you were cranking out Monday through Thursday last week when you were apparently trying to become a professional triathlete overnight. The lift days landing back-to-back on Thursday and Friday is the one genuinely smart thing here, and frankly it is very on-brand that the weights got done even when almost everything else fell away — turns out the chest and shoulders are non-negotiable, which, yes, obviously.
metrics_commentary: Thirteen-point-three kilometres in one session and three thousand metres in the pool — that is not a casual week, that is a man who showed up and actually did the thing. The HRV coming in at 62 against a seven-day average of 51 is your body basically winking at you, telling you recovery is trending in exactly the right direction, which means you can stop being precious about rest and start being ambitious about what comes next. A VO2 Max of 56.7 is genuinely strong — that is a cardiovascular engine that would make a lot of guys deeply jealous, and you built it, which is very sexy of you. The one thing worth watching is that the run-walk distance is sitting across a single session, so spreading the movement out a little more through the week would keep that HRV climbing instead of spiking and dipping.
gym_commentary: Two sessions out of four this week, which means half the programme got done and the other half did not, and we are going to be honest about that while also noting that the bench press crept up to a 35.5 kg estimated one-rep max, which is a new personal record, and that is genuinely something. Volume came in at 13,705 kg against last week's 20,148 kg, so the body of work was lighter, partly because the sessions just weren't there. What matters now is that the strength numbers — 168 kg on leg press, nearly 108 on the trap bar deadlift — are holding, the chest is moving in the right direction, and a guy who shows up twice when life gets in the way and still hits a PR is not a guy who is losing ground. Get back in there next week, all four sessions, and let's see what that bench does.
